speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, this is tape 2, interviewing Malka Baran. Is there something that comes to your memory that you would like to say that we didn't cover in the previous tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, not especially. I think I covered things that I remembered vividly. Uh, maybe it would be important to know that, uh, before Passover, I think, one of my aunts had a bakery-- a kosher one. And I remember my mother-- although we didn't keep a kosher home-- And other women from our, uh, area were baking macaroons and cakes for Passover there. And the smells and the busy women, I remember. I recall, of course, playing around there with my brother-- not helping much, but looking. And I realize that those memories are the ones that remain with you-- the textures and the smells and the visual effects of women, Jewish women, baking for the holiday. So I guess it had that, uh, aroma of Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were speaking about your father's, uh, printing business. Was it a big business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very big. It was a small business. He had a printing machine and a rolling paper machine. And, uh, he set this-- the type by himself using a pinsette, um, and little letters. I remember helping him sometimes doing that. He had one helper there. And that was about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big, uh-- was it-- how big of a room-- of a facility was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, I would say, uh, as big as this room-- our living room, not more than that. It was in the same building. I guess it was once an apartment. And he rented.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was it from your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same building, downstairs. It was in our walking distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things start going, uh, bad before the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- in '39, I remember the Germans occupied Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was a very-- Poland, probably. A very short while it took, maybe one day. We were-- we heard some, uh, shooting, bombs. And we ran into basements. We hid for a while. And then we came out, and it was over. The occupation was already taking place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What part of the year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, I guess September-- some-- sometimes in the fall. I'm not very keen on the exact dates. I did not record them then. And it was all event after event. And it was shocking and disturbing. And I don't know how people remember those dates. I don't. But I know it was in '39, because we-- uh, we were-- our regular life stopped. School was closed. And teachers were disappearing after a while. And, uh, we saw Germans-- office-- uh, German soldiers, of course, in the streets. And very soon, one decree after another appeared. And that's what I remembered how the life changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- what was it like for you to see German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at first, uh, we took it as a fact. Poland was taken over by Germany. And we hoped that everything will be OK and somehow we will continue our normal lives. But for me, very soon it appeared that things were happening, uh, very, uh-- in-- into a negative direction. First of all, after a certain while, we were, uh, ordered to wear stars on our arms, those, uh, yellow stars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ordered this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German occupants-- occupiers. And then my father's machinery was taken away by the Germans. The ghetto was erected in, I believe, '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were still living in your homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: We still lived in our homes. Uh, my father still worked until they took the machinery away from him. And then he was, uh, working. They sent us to work in other places for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what my father did. But, uh, [pauses for 4 seconds] m-- my, uh-- I was sent to certain places with young girls to clean, to wash the windows in the ghetto. Uh, at first, we got a sort of bread-- uh, those, uh, coupons for food-- those that worked. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Wh-- when was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: --ghetto established?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '41, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how-- how did your family manage until 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess they were doing some business on the side or having some money before. I don't know, uh, how we lived. But life was not the same. It was, uh, more restricted, more limited. Food was, uh, plainer. Uh, clothes were not purchased. You know, we-- we felt-- then, after a while, the children were not allowed to play outside. Certain hours we were kept home. And, uh, that's more or less how it started for us. Of course, the adults knew much more than that. There were some rumors. But from what I heard from, uh, people around me, they were hoping that things will change, that it will stabilize. Nobody assumed-- no-- nobody thought that things will come to a-- because the Germans did it very slowly and slyly. And, uh, we will give you work. We'll give you, uh, food-- those that work. They sent people to various other places to work out of town. But slowly things changed for us, too. The doctors that we used to go to suddenly disappeared. We came, for example, for a checkup. And the doors were barred. And the explanation was they were sent out of town to work. Nobody knew where this was. Obviously, they were sent or killed, or to other places.

speaker: interviewer
text: [DRINKS] Uh, can you tell me, how old were you around that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- uh, it started in '39, uh, when I was 12. So I must have been 13, 14-- 14 when things became bad-- 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother was already bar mitzvahed by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, he was being prepared by that, uh, rabbi who used to come. We never reached the bar mitzvah. He was killed before he became bar mitzvahed. But so he was just preparing for it. I don't remember when his bar mitzvah date was supposed to be. But I know that we didn't have bar mitzvah. I celebrated my last birthday with my family, which was not a celebration, but they bought me a gift-- I'll show you that later-- and on which it is written, uh, the date of my birth. And that was my 15th birthday. It was shortly before-- January my birthday was. And the-- and they were destroyed in, uh-- around Rosh Hashanah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your brother killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me go chronologically. Um, one day, early, early in the morning, my parents woke me up. That I remember very vividly. I'll describe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, that must have been '43, or '44 maybe. I don't know. Uh, my parents woke us up very early. It was still dark outside-- very quietly. And s-- we saw that something is wrong. They told us to dress. They gave us a lot of clothes, layers of clothes, put on us coats and sweaters underneath and jackets. And they dressed themselves. And I walked over to the window. And I saw, in the dark, SS men lining up the street a-- across the window. And there were no people. So we knew something is wrong. And we knew from our parents' faces that something was wrong. And soon enough, my father took some-- I remember him giving my mother some gold coins, some money in gold that he probably saved. She put it somewhere on her body. He put some on his. I was just watching. They didn't tell us anything. And soon enough, there was a knock, a very sharp knock on the front-- on our door. And the door was pushed open. And the SS men, with the guns, came in and shouted, raus, raus, very, um, angrily.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that-- what does 'raus' mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out, out, out, out! And they chased us out of our home, out to the street. And on the street, we saw, at the same time, families from other homes around us, from other apartments. And they were all pushed into the middle of the street where the cars usually go. And we were gathered there. And we never saw our home again-- our apartment. That was the last time that we saw our apartment. We were standing-- they formed us in lines of five. And that was women and children and older people. And all-- the whole population of the ghetto-- it was at that time the large ghetto-- was lined up in fives. We were four. With us was the girl who worked with us. She was the fifth one. And when everybody was chased out of their homes, they started marching us to a certain market, a big sp-- uh, space. And from there was the selection, the Akt-- the s-- the choosing. We didn't know anything. I didn't know anything at that time. My parents didn't know because we didn't experience that before. It was something sudden. The Germans stood on both sides with the guns against us. We were all civilians. It was early in the morning. The action was very, very fast. Out, out-- screaming, shouting at us. People were huddled together in fives. Then we came to that space. And I remember the German said, rechts, links, rechts, links-- right, left, right, left. And it happened so that my mother and the girl who worked with us were separated from us. And my father and I and my brother were put on the other side. I remember clearly my father r-- tried to run to the mother-- to my mother side. And the German hit him with a gun in the back and pushed him back. So he came back to us. And my mother was taken away. Those that were on one side were taken away. Those that were on the out-- outside-- other side, we were taken to a place called metallurgia, which was a big, big court-- inner court of a very large factory of metal. There we gather. And we were already separated from my mother. That was the f-- first major shock for me, because I saw what was happening. And my mother was taken away. We never saw her again. And obviously, she was taken-- that Aktion was taken-- later, we found out-- to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother and I and my father remained in that metallurgia, in that metal outfit with many other Jews who remained. And we stayed there for a long time. I don't remember how much. That's when my memory started sort of not functioning anymore. Then I didn't know. But now I know. Uh, at a certain point, I was told by my friends that from that metallurgia, we were put in a small ghetto, in a limited ghetto where we were concentrated. And I don't remember-- I have a very severe memory block from then on. I don't remember where we lived together. I remember that we went to work again for the Germans. I remember going to wash windows, clean the houses. My father was sent with my brother to other work. They used to come back. I have no recollection whatsoever where we lived, how big a room it was, with whom we lived. That is blank. Uh, at a certain time, my father and my brother did not come back. And later on, I was told by people who saw that, who worked with them, that they were shot. They were working in the railroad, carrying on their arms those long rails that they put on the track. And they were shot in the back, both of them. That's all I know. And from then on, until we were taken to concentration camp, I have a completely memory-- complete memory block. My girlfriends who survived-- one of them-- and stayed with me throughout that period from that moment on-- when my father and brother didn't come back-- to the time when we were taken to a concentration camp, they tried to describe the room for me, what we did. She even said that we were studying a little bit together uh, after work. I don't recall any of this. The first thing that I remember was when we were taken to HASAG, to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: That-- what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '43, beginning-- spring of '43. And there I remained until the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, let's go back to 1941. You said that the ghetto was-- in Czestochowa-- created in 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right, the large-- the large ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, can you describe, uh, your journ-- your trip, your-- your-- how you went to the ghetto, what happened? Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't because I don't-- I don't remember. That's when my memory does not, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: You do not remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- obviously-- I don't remember anything from that period. Um, ob-- obviously, I was traumatized by-- by my mother's disappearance, by, uh, everything that took place. I don't recall any of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: From 1941 until you went to the camp, uh, you don't remember the details?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 1940-- the place where we lived, I don't remember, after our apartment. Uh, when my father and brother came back every day, I don't remember where we were until I went to camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you rem-- des-- do you remember the-- that little ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, do you remember going to that concentration camp? What was the name of the camp? Or the-- or the labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a labor camp in Czestochowa. On the outskirts of Czestochowa, there was a large, uh, factory prior to that metal factory. The Germans, uh, made it a factory to clean the shells of the anti-aircraft ammunition. Those were larger-- that size. And they came from the front. And there were machines at which I worked, all kinds of machines. My machine was sorting out the damaged shells and straightening them, sorting those that were impossible to repair. And those that were repairable, the machines were correcting them. And I was at the sorting machine. That's where I work. I remember the first night we came there, we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, they-- they chased us there. The Germans-- we didn't go freely. The Germans took us from the ghetto, closed the small ghetto, obviously. Uh, put s-- some people were sent away to either, uh, camps-- uh, death camps or whatever. And some people were taken to that fabric-- to that, uh, factory, which was HASAG. Hugo Schneider Aktiengesellschaft, that was the name-- German name. And obviously, we were very small in numbers. The rest was liquidated. The ghetto was liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- did you walk there from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They forced us. I was passive. I didn't do anything throughout the war to save my life or to run away or to do something. I was a completely-- I guess I was-- I was 15. I was shocked. I was, uh, numb. I was just passive, doing what I was told to do, as my friends and everybody else did-- except for those few that I don't know about that you hear from history that ran away. I also remember one event that was very important. It must have been either when they liquidated the small ghetto or when they took us out of homes and gathered us on a marketplace. I don't recall which one it was. But I remember standing there with other Jewish people, surrounded by German SS men with guns. And I remember a small group of Jewish men, young men start-- a-- attacked the Germans from somewhere that was, uh, probably from hiding-- with ammunition, attacked the Germans. And they were completely subdued immediately. But the Germans started shooting all around us. And next to me, a person was shot. The other side, a person was shot. This was like a picture, a shocking moment out of nowhere. And I was standing there. And I-- I survived. That's what I remember. What happened after that-- I remember seeing babies-- this was a horrible thing-- thrown against the wall. And they're-- they're killed. And they're-- after they attack. And their bodies were thrown on the machines. Uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES] You saw-- you saw--

speaker: interviewee
text: --after that, I don't remember anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw a baby being shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not one. We were all together standing. And after this event, Germans just shoot all around and throw babies against the wall. And it was a whole terrible commotion. And of course, after that, I don't remember anything. But that must have been either, uh, before liquidation of the small ghetto or before-- after taking us out from homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, you were speaking of the small, uh, ghetto. Do you remember what it looked like? [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have no idea. Complete block of memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, do you remember, when you were going to this factory, were you walking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were-- we were in lines marching. On both side were SS men. And we were sort of led fast into that, uh, big building. And we all, at night, uh, laid down on the floors, everybody together on, uh bare floors, until the next morning when we were assigned to our barracks. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the concentration camp, we called it. Uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a concentration camp? Or was it a labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was a labor camp. Uh, we called it concentration camp because it concentrated the Jews in that area. But there were no gas chambers there. And there were no, uh-- uh, there was some killing there, you know, by shooting, but no-- no-- just labor camp. We worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in barracks? Or what was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that I can describe. Uh, interestingly enough, that I remember very vividly, from that moment on. We were living in barracks. The barracks had women separated from men. There were barracks for men and barracks for women. Uh, in my barrack where I lived, there were 16-- there was a long board like you saw in the Washington museum, if you went there-- those long, wooden-- uh, I don't know what they call them in English. Uh, not beds, but wooden boards. One's on top of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bunk beds?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bunks. Not bunks, but-- but there were bunks. Wood, on top of us another wood, and on top of us a third one. There were three layers. I was on the bottom. My friends were on the top, whom I met in concentration-- in that camp, not before. We lived together. And there were a few hundreds of women in that barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there a gate-- a-- uh, barbed wire?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was within-- yes. That was within the camp-- within the factory, uh, area. There was a large, large area outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, we slept on those bunks without any bedding. We didn't have any pillows or sheets or anything. We had, after a while, a lot of, uh, animal friends-- bugs and, uh-- and fleas, and, uh-- what do you call them? Uh, lice, bed bugs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember sleeping covered with my coat, uh, which I left-- by the way, I left my apartment house in a skirt and a top and a sweater and a coat, a very warm coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES] Uh, this was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Blue coat that was from home.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the little ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was from our home, when we never returned. Everybody was dressed. That coat served me as a cover on my bunk. Um, we slept in our clothes because there was no change of clothes. Um, next to me was-- uh, were two sisters. One of them was a lunatic. Uh, when the moon was out-- I remember vividly-- she got up from her bunk. She walked. She recited history in Polish-- Polish history, like a professor. And she walked, and sometimes she walked out. Sometimes she walked back. And she slept right next to me. So it was another experience that I remember. In the morning, early, a German came to take us out of the, uh, barrack. We were counted. And we were led to work-- to machines where we worked. At the big, large hall where the machines were being, uh, worked on were three-- three Germans-- uh, two German women, very vicious, very mean. They supervised the work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these kapos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, no, they were the Germans, the German women, not Jewish. Uh, there was a Jewish kapo also. But I don't recall anything wrong or drastic per-- personally to us, uh, that I knew of. But the German women were vicious. And there was an office within that hall with a glass top, uh, all around the office. And the bottom was covered, not-- not, uh, see-through. And there was a German officer supervising everything. And we called him 'the American,' because he happened to be-- uh, he showed himself to be a human being. Uh, when he called someone to his office under the pretext that he's going to punish or scream-- shouted and screamed. But under the cover, he gave them sandwiches. So we had a-- sort of a-- uh, we-- we called him, in secret, 'the American.' Uh, but the women were very mean. And I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES] How--

speaker: interviewee
text: --incidents that, uh, they were screaming at prisoners. They were shouting. They were, uh, hitting, you know, in the face. And there was one row of machines that had a wires brush turning very, very fast. And, uh, the-- the shells were put-- the anti-aircraft shells were put into the brush by the prisoners to clean them inside, to polish them. And I remember one woman prisoner, who was obviously tired, fell asleep or bent her head. And her whole hair, scalp was torn by the machine. So we worked there. And I'll come now to the, uh, story of my little heart and chain, which I'll show you later. I wore-- my watch and my ring were taken by the Germans off at the time where they took us out from home. They just checked everything and tore everything they saw. Give me the watch. Give me the ring. And, fast, fast, fast. So of course, we gave everything. Under all the layer of clothes, which they didn't have time to look for, obviously, I had a heart, that was given-- a golden heart that was given to me by my parents on my last birthday on a golden chain. When I came to camp, I wore a skirt. And somehow I-- I made a little, uh, tiny entering hole in that, uh, waistline-- waistband and put the heart and the chain in it to keep it. It was my only thing from home. We have no pictures, nothing. Um, and during the work-- labor camp, on, uh-- on my machine where I worked, there were three people working. One of them was a Polish woman. Muszka, her name was. And she used to come to that-- Muszka, M-U-S-Z-K-A. She used to come to camp daily. I remember she used to bring us all kinds of things-- little pins for hair, little piece of bread when she could. She was afraid, but when she could. We gave-- I gave her that chain for a b-- for a-- for a whole bread. But the heart I kept.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the chain is gone. That whole piece of bread I shared with my friends. And we ate it, of course, at one sitting because we were very hungry. We're coming to the end of this tape. We'll continue.
